data.title: Photronics: Growth Ahead At A Deeply Reasonable Price
data.description:
Photronics Inc. (NASDAQ:PLAB) presents a compelling investment opportunity as an undervalued, critical player in the semiconductor supply chain. While the semiconductor industry is currently at the trough of a cyclical downturn, all signs are pointing to a potential recovery in late 2025 or early 2026 thanks to technological advancements in the AI sector and normalizing inventory levels after a long period of oversupply.

Although Photronics is largely exposed to mainstream IC applications, its high-end IC segment has been gaining traction over the past few quarters due to node migration to smaller geometries. This migration is mainly driven by companies focusing on efficiency and shrinking their devices.

The ongoing node migration is also creating opportunities for the company to increase its content in AI applications, which is well-positioned to grow rapidly over the coming years, especially with Nvidia’s 800 V HVDC data center architecture. At the same time, Photronics is well-positioned to gain a stable long-term revenue stream from Tesla’s (TSLA) recent deal with Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) to manufacture its next-generation AI6 chip in Samsung’s Texas fab, considering that Samsung was the company’s second largest customer in FY 2024.

Furthermore, Photronics is less-exposed to the tariff risk, despite depending on China, Taiwan, and South Korea for a majority of its revenue, thanks to its diversified geographical footprint, with manufacturing facilities in each of its main regions. With that in mind, the company is investing $200 million to expand its US manufacturing capacity to capitalize on market opportunities in the US driven by AI and HPC applications. The company also intends to upgrade its Asian facilities to expand its offerings to cater to smaller nodes in its largest markets.

Photronics’ significant investments in its facilities showcase its financial strength. Although the company’s revenues have been declining since last year due to the ongoing semiconductor downturn, it is still a profitable, free-cash-flow-generating business, with a fortress-like balance sheet. In fact, the company holds 42% of its market cap in cash with negligible debt on its balance sheet.

Given the company’s strong fundamentals, a potential semiconductor recovery in the short-term, and its expanding opportunity in high-margin AI and HPC applications, I’m rating Photronics as a buy with a price target of $59 by 2028, implying 153% upside from current levels.

Business Overview

Photronics is a leading global manufacturer of photomasks, which are highly precise quartz or glass plates containing microscopic images of electronic circuits. These photomasks are crucial components in semiconductors’ integrated circuits (ICs) and flat panel displays (FPDs), by transferring circuit patterns onto wafers for ICs and onto FPDs during the fabrication process. Photronics’ business is divided into 2 segments, ICs and FPDs, both of which include high-end photomasks and mainstream photomasks.

For ICs, high-end photomasks serve IC nodes smaller or equal to 28 nanometers, which are suitable for high-growth applications such as HPC, AI and ML, flagship smartphones, and high-end consumer electronics. Meanwhile, mainstream IC photomasks serve IC nodes greater than 28 nanometers which have end markets such as automotive electronics, industrial control systems, IoT devices, and regular consumer electronics.

For FPDs, high-end photomasks include AMOLED, G10.5+, and LTPS photomasks. These photomasks are included in applications such as high-end smartphones, HD and large display TVs, wearable devices, automotive displays, and commercial displays. Meanwhile, mainstream FPD photomasks include G8 and smaller photomasks, which serve end markets such as PC monitors, laptop displays, and mainstream smartphones and tablets.

The IC segment is Photronics’ largest segment, representing 73-74% of its total revenues, while the FPD segment represents around 26-27% of its total revenue. Accordingly, Photronics’ future growth is mainly dependent on the IC segment, especially high-end photomasks since they carry higher ASPs given the level of precision required in the applications they are included in.

With that in mind, Photronics operates in a highly specialized and concentrated market with high entry barriers, mainly due to the capital intensive nature of this business. Currently, the company’s largest competitors are Dai Nippon Printing (OTCPK:DNPCF), Toppan Photomasks (OTCPK:TONPF), and HOYA Corporation (OTCPK:HOCPF), all of which are Japanese companies. At the same time, the company faces competition from smaller local manufacturers in China, making Photronics the leading player in the photomasks industry in the Western world.

Despite this status, Photronics generates the vast majority of its revenues outside the US and Europe, with Taiwan, China, and South Korea being its largest markets, respectively. In Q3 FY 2025, the company generated 77.8% of its revenues from outside the Western world, up from 77% in the prior year. The company also generated 79.4% of its YTD revenues from outside the western world compared to 79.2% in the same prior year period.

Given this reliance on Asian markets for a significant chunk of its revenues, Photronics has an impressive global manufacturing footprint. The company has 11 manufacturing facilities strategically located in its largest markets to offer localized service and short lead times to its customers. These facilities are located in the US, Europe, China, Taiwan, and South Korea.

Photronics’ emphasis on localized services is a major tailwind to its business, especially with the ongoing tariff uncertainty, as it minimizes imports to tariffed countries. This significantly reduces Photronics’ exposure to tariff-related duties and supply chain disruptions.

Financial Overview

Top-Line Analysis

Given that ICs represent the largest revenue stream for Photronics, the company is impacted by the general conditions of the semiconductor industry since IC sales are dependent on new wafer buildouts. As such, Photronics’ revenues have been declining since FY 2024, in line with the broader industry.

In FY 2024, the company reported a 2.8% YoY revenue decline from $892 million to $866.9 million, driven by a 2% decline in IC revenues from $651.3 million to $638.2 million and a 5% decline in FPD revenues from $240.8 million to $228.8 million. The revenue slowdown has caused Photronics’ gross margin to contract YoY from 37.7% to 36.4% in FY 2024.

However, a deeper look shows that high-end ICs saw 17% YoY growth during that period from $194.9 million to $228.5 million to represent 35.8% of IC revenues compared to 29.9% in FY 2023.

The growth Photronics is seeing in high-end ICs is an extremely promising sign as it highlights its opportunity in the AI sector due to the applications of this segment. The company has previously highlighted this opportunity in its FQ 2 2025 earnings call, stating that it is seeing strong foundry demand for 22 and 28 nanometer photomask products in Asia.

This opportunity could be a major growth tailwind in the coming years, as I will discuss later in this report, especially with high-end ICs carrying a higher ASP profile compared to the rest of Photronics’ product line-up.

Bottom-Line Analysis

In terms of profitability, Photronics remains a profitable business, albeit impacted by the shrinking revenues caused by the semiconductor industry’s slowdown. In FY 2024, the company reported an EBIT of $221.6 million compared to $253 million in the prior year, at an EBIT margin of 25.6% compared to 28.4% in FY 2023.

This was mainly caused by the revenue slowdown as well as higher operating costs, highlighted by a 21.4% increase in R&D spending.

As the company is now expected to report around $80 million in CapEx in FQ4 2025, it is likely to report negative free cash flow during the upcoming quarter. This trend could continue into FY 2026 since the company announced plans to upgrade its Asian facilities to support the production of smaller node photomasks as well as replacing its equipment that reached their end-of-life. According to the Q3 earnings call, this CapEx cycle is expected to last 3 years, which could see its FCF margins under pressure during that period.

Despite the short-term disruption to its free cash flow generation, Photronics’ expansion plan comes at an opportune time to capitalize on the reshoring of semiconductor manufacturing in the US, as part of the Trump administration’s “America First” policy. Similarly, the company’s investments in upgrading its Asian facilities allows it to increase its AI-related business in Asia. Accordingly, these investments could see Photronics’ margins expand in the long-term as its high-end IC photomask sales increase.

Semiconductor Recovery

As mentioned earlier, Photronics is impacted by the cyclicality of the semiconductor industry since photomasks are an enabler technology directly tied to the production and design cycles of chips. In fact, photomasks are fundamental for wafer fabrication since every new chip design and every significant upgrade to an existing chip design requires a new set of photomasks. Accordingly, when demand for electronic devices increases, and chip manufacturers ramp up their production, their need for photomasks increases, which is the main tailwind to Photronics’ business.

In prior coverages on Aehr Test Systems (AEHR) and Axcelis Technologies (ACLS), I discussed the potential for a cyclical semiconductor recovery later this year or early 2026. First of all, Photronics’ mainstream segment stands to be a major beneficiary from a potential rebound in consumer electronics.

This is directly tied to Deutsche Bank Research forecasting 6%, 5%, and 4% growth in computing, consumer electronics, and automotive sectors, respectively, in 2025. In this way, Photronics is likely to see a rebound in “mainstream” photomasks revenues in Q1 FY 2026, since its fiscal year ends in October of any given CY. This forecast is supported by data from McKinsey showing that inventories are declining to more normalized levels, in line with pre-pandemic years.

Moreover, early offshoots of this potential recovery were seen in Q1 wafer shipment data from SEMI, showing that total silicon wafer shipments increased by 2.2% YoY during the quarter. This trend can also be seen in SEMI’s Q2 wafer shipment data which showed a 9.6% YoY increase in global silicon wafer shipments. Q2 shipments were also 14.9% higher than the first quarter, indicating signs of recovery in sectors beyond AI, which further supports my conviction of a broader industry rebound in late 2025/early 2026.

Furthermore, the SEMI World Fab Forecast Report showed that a wave of investments in wafer fabrication capacity expansion is underway, with 32 fabs launching this year and construction on 18 new fabs is expected to start this year, the majority of which will be online in 2026 and 2027. These growing investments, mainly driven by AI-fueled demand, are especially a promising sign for Photronics since they create demand for its most advanced high-end IC photomasks, which carry higher ASPs and gross margins.

AI Opportunity

On that note, the expected recovery in high-end applications is mainly driven by the growth of the AI ecosystem, positioning Photronics to capture a significant opportunity in this sector thanks to its fundamental role in the fabrication of advanced chips.

These AI applications, in areas such as data centers, HPC, and edge AI, demand complex, smaller, faster, and more power-efficient chips, which usually come in nodes equal or smaller than 7 nanometers. This expected growth in the AI ecosystem will also be supported by Nvidia’s next-generation 800 V HVDC data center architecture, which has higher semiconductor content compared to current data center architectures.

Given the small size of these advanced chips, they require highly precise photomasks, which is in line with Photronics expertise. As chipmakers continue to introduce more advanced chip designs to push AI advancements to the edge, they require more photomasks per design.

At the same time, as these designs become more intricate, they also lead to a higher ASP per photomask set due to the level of complexity of these chips. This combination of higher ASPs and more photomask content per chip design could be long-term tailwinds to Photronics’ revenues and profitability.

One such opportunity Photronics has is Samsung’s latest $16.5 billion deal with Tesla to manufacture the latter’s AI6 chip in Samsung’s Taylor, Texas fab. The deal is a multi-year agreement extending through 2033, and Elon Musk stated that the $16.5 billion figure is “just the bare minimum”. While no timeline was provided for AI6 chip production, analysts expect production to commence in 2027-2028, as Tesla’s AI5 chips are set to be produced at the end of 2026, as confirmed by Musk in Tesla’s latest earnings call.

The AI6 chip is expected to be the foundation of Tesla’s future EVs and the core of its self-driving vehicles and Optimus humanoid robots. This could be a sign of a significant period of production, driving demand for photomasks and positioning Photronics to capture a significant market opportunity.

The reason Photronics is likely to be a winner from this deal is that Samsung was its second largest customer in FY 2024, as the company’s latest 10-K filing stated the following (emphasis added):

During 2024, we sold our products to approximately 675 customers. Revenue from United Microelectronics Corp. Co., Ltd. accounted for approximately 15%, 14% and 15% of our total revenues in 2024, 2023 and 2022, respectively, and revenue from Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. accounted for approximately 12%, 10% and 11% of our total revenues in those respective years. In addition, revenue from Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation accounted for approximately 9%, 13% and 5% of our total revenues in 2024, 2023 and 2022, respectively

In this way, the deal could provide Photronics with a stable, long-term, high-margin revenue stream given that the AI6 chip will be in 4 and 3 nanometer nodes, with the potential to further shrink to 2 nanometer nodes in the future.

Node Migration

Beyond this opportunity in core processors, Photronics has other opportunities in supporting the AI ecosystem in advanced packaging and interconnects. With ever-increasing AI workloads, the need for fast interconnects, including silicon photonics and advanced packaging solutions, is also increasing. This is driving demand for photomasks since they are crucial for creating the patterns required for these technologies, which allow multiple dies to be stacked and interconnected to improve the performance of AI systems.

The increasing complexity and demand for advanced chips are directly leading to a phenomenon that is set to be a major growth driver for Photronics over the coming years, node migration. The node migration in the IC market is driven by the continuous shrinking of transistor sizes and the advancement to newer and smaller process technologies to benefit from the performance and efficiency gains offered by advanced nodes.

Currently, the IC market is in the midst of a significant node migration that is expected to continue and even accelerate in the coming years due to demand for advanced nodes in the rapidly growing AI and HPC sectors. At the same time, node migration is also driven by the EV and autonomous driving sectors that require high-performance chips for battery management and ADAS.

With chip designs continuously shrinking and becoming more complex, they require more mask layers per chip and higher precision. This in turn creates a significant market opportunity for Photronics thanks to its strength in high-end IC photomasks.

To that end, Photronics is planning capability extensions to its Asian facilities to allow for the production of 8-nanometer and 6-nanometer photomasks, with a target of realizing revenues from this endeavor by late FY 2027 or early FY 2028. By extending its offerings to cater to smaller nodes, the company could gain significant content from customers developing high-end processors for end-markets such as memory, EV, and communications.

With that in mind, Photronics is already capable of producing 8 and 6-nanometer photomasks in its Idaho facility in the US, being the only US company able to achieve this feat onshore, as mentioned in the Q3 earnings call. This provides it with a significant edge with US hyperscalers developing their own AI chips.

Revenue Projections

To forecast Photronics' revenues through 2028, I considered the impact of the current cyclical trough in the semiconductor industry, a potential recovery in late CY 2025, rapid growth in the AI and HPC sectors, a moderate growth in EVs and autonomous vehicles, and Tesla’s deal with Samsung. Based on these factors, my revenue targets for Photronics are as follows.

For FY 2025, I’m forecasting a 3.3% YoY revenue decline to $838.5 million, reflecting the ongoing semiconductor downturn, especially given Photronics fiscal year ends in October, the first month of CY Q4. As such, the company has limited potential to benefit from a broader recovery in late CY 2025. This forecast is also consistent with Photronics’ FQ4 2025 revenue guidance between $201 to $209 million.

For FY 2026, I’m forecasting 5% YoY revenue growth to $880.4 million, expecting that Photronics fully captures the early stages of the broader semiconductor industry recovery. I expect this growth to be driven by demand for high-end IC photomasks and a rebound in mainstream photomasks as consumer electronics and automotive sectors improve.

For FY 2027, I’m forecasting 6% YoY revenue growth, accelerating from FY 2026, to $933.3 million as node migration intensifies due to demand for high-performance chips in EVs and autonomous driving. This forecast also takes into consideration initial revenue contributions from the Samsung-Tesla deal for the production of AI6 chips.

For FY 2028, I’m forecasting 8% YoY revenue growth to $1 billion. This accelerated growth reflects the full impact of rapid node migration in AI and HPC applications, supported by Photronics’ finalized capability extensions in Asian facilities, driven by widespread adoption of Nvidia’s 800 V HVDC data center architecture. This forecast also reflects higher revenues from the Samsung-Tesla deal as AI6 chip production ramps up.

Valuation

Given this revenue forecast, I will be forecasting Photronics’ EBIT through FY 2028 to reach my price target for the stock as I will be taking an EV/EBIT approach. The reasons behind this methodology are that Photronics is already a mature, profitable, free-cash-flow-generating company, while also taking the high depreciation costs associated with its business into consideration.

Accordingly, I’m forecasting Photronics’ EBIT and EBIT margins through FY 2028 as follows.

For FY 2025, I’m forecasting an EBIT of $199.1 million reflecting a 23.7% EBIT margin, anticipating EBIT margin compression from 25.6% reported in FY 2024. This forecast is consistent with management’s FQ4 EBIT margin guidance between 20-22%. That said, it should be noted that Photronics has a history of beating its own guidance, as witnessed by its FQ3 earnings results compared to the guidance issued in FQ2.

For FY 2026, I’m forecasting an EBIT of $224.5 million, reflecting YoY EBIT margin expansion to 25.5%. This forecast is driven by the potential semiconductor industry recovery, combined with improved operating leverage and a growing contribution from higher gross margin high-end IC segment.

For FY 2027, I’m forecasting continued EBIT margin expansion to 27.5%, leading to an EBIT of $256.6 million. This forecast is supported by accelerating revenue growth from node migration and the initial ramp-up of high-margin AI opportunities, including the Samsung-Tesla deal, further improving operating leverage.

For FY 2028, I’m forecasting $292.3 million in EBIT, reflecting a 29% EBIT margin. This forecast is driven by robust revenue growth and a favorable mix towards high-end IC photomasks, which command higher ASPs and gross margins. The EBIT margin forecast is also slightly higher than the 28.4% EBIT margin reported in FY 2023, where Photronics reported all-time-record revenues, showcasing improved profitability from advanced technologies.

At its current share price of $23.41, Photronics has a market cap of $1.38 billion, which translates to an EV of $805.5 million after considering its net cash position of $575.8 million. As such, the stock is trading at an EV/EBIT multiple of 4.04 relative to my FY 2025 EBIT forecast.

Photronics’ current valuation represents a discount to its historical EV/EBIT multiples, as its 3-year median stands at 5.042, 5-year median stands at 6.007, and 7-year median stands at 8.183. The gap between Photronics’ current EV/EBIT multiple and its historical levels is primarily due to the broader semiconductor industry downturn.

However, the industry appears to be heading back to growth in late 2025 or early 2026 which can drive a rerating in the coming quarters. With that in mind, the YChart below shows that Photronics traded at an EV/EBIT multiple around 14 during the previous industry peak in 2020-2021.

In this way, the upcoming cycle could see the stock return to trade at a similar EV/EBIT multiple, considering Photronics’ high-growth catalysts related to the Tesla-Samsung deal, node migration, and the full realization of its capability extensions in Asia. All of these factors are major tailwinds to the company’s high-margin high-end IC segment due to the level of specialization required for edge AI chips.

At the same time, Photronics’ largest competitors in the photomask market, Dai Nippon, Toppan, and HOYA, are trading at EV/EBIT multiples of 11.95, 14.30, and 25.17, respectively.

Despite this, my target EV/EBIT multiple for Photronics is 10, a discount compared to its largest competitors since they all have diversified businesses, offering products far beyond photomasks. At the same time, my target multiple is consistent with Photronics’ historical multiple during growth cycles in the semiconductor industry.

Based on this target multiple, my long-term price target for Photronics is $59.30, representing a potential upside of 153%.

Risks

The first risk to consider before investing in Photronics is cyclicality of the semiconductor industry. While signs are pointing to a recovery in late CY 2025 or early CY 2026, a prolonged inventory correction, or slower recovery in consumer electronics and automotive sectors, could lead to slower growth compared to my forecast. This could have an impact on Photronics’ profitability and in turn its valuation, even at the stock’s current muted valuation metrics.

Moreover, a slower recovery could delay transitions to smaller nodes, potentially leading to a slower-than-expected ramp-up in AI and HPC applications. In this way, the forecasted demand for Photronics’ high-end photomasks may not materialize as expected, impacting both revenue growth and EBIT margin expansion.

Another main risk to consider is related to Photronics’ and Samsung’s execution. With Photronics investing $200 million to expand its US manufacturing capabilities, project delays, cost overruns, and yield challenges could be a drag on Photronics’ profitability and cash flow generation, and could also impact its opportunities that were the main driver for this expansion.

This is also relevant with Samsung following its deal with Tesla. If the production ramp for the AI6 chip is delayed beyond the expected 2027-2028 timeline or if Samsung has yield issues in the Taylor fab, the anticipated revenues and margin benefits from this deal to Photronics may not fully materialize or be delayed. This would be a major headwind to the bullish thesis on Photronics.

Finally, the majority of Photronics’ revenues are concentrated within a limited customer base, despite selling to 675 customers in FY 2024. As is, the company’s top 5 customers accounted for 50%, 51%, and 45% of its total revenues in FY 2024, 2023, and 2022, respectively. This exposes Photronics to lose a significant portion of its revenues if any of these customers reduces their photomasks orders or even moves their photomasks production in-house. This could have a devastating impact on Photronics’ financials.

Conclusion

In summary, Photronics presents an interesting investment opportunity at its current valuation. Despite maintaining its profitability and cash flow generation during an industry-wide downturn, its stock is trading at a significant discount to other photomask manufacturers. With a semiconductor industry recovery expected in late CY 2025 to early CY 2026, Photronics has a significant opportunity to return to growth in FY 2026, especially with the momentum its high-end IC photomasks are seeing during the current cyclical trough.

Furthermore, the ongoing node migration in the IC market driven by technology advancements in the AI and HPC sectors, could be a long-term growth tailwind to Photronics. As is, the company likely has direct exposure to the $16.5 billion Samsung-Tesla deal given that Samsung was its second largest customer in FY 2024.

Given these tailwinds, I’m forecasting Photronics to grow its top-line at a 6.3% CAGR from FY 2025 to FY 2028, mainly driven by its high-end IC segment. This growth opportunity could be a major tailwind to Photronics’ gross margins and EBIT margins, potentially supporting a rerating as the semiconductor industry’s next growth phase approaches. In light of these factors, and the stock’s steep discount compared to historical trends and industry peers, I’m rating Photronics as a buy with a price target of $59 by 2028, representing 153% upside from its current valuation.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. data.title: 2 High-Yield, Investment-Grade Bonds For Your Retirement Portfolio
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities.

The stock market is an institution filled with drama. However, unlike the theater that we enjoy for entertainment, this drama has money at stake, leading to heightened emotions among participants. Financial turmoil can arise in mysterious and unpredictable ways; vulnerabilities range from operational shifts to market risks. Economic events can act as the catalyst to fuel volatility in your financial well-being.

Every investment carries risks, but the level of exposure varies across the company’s capital structure. By investing higher up in the capital structure, you can immunize your investment from most risks while keeping a steady stream of income pouring cash into your account. Securities that carry investment-grade ratings are perceived to come with a certain degree of safety due to strong operating fundamentals, financial position, and outlook, as determined by independent agencies. The investment-grade status indicates a lower risk of default and a stronger ability to meet financial obligations and commitments.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we maintain a diversified portfolio of over 50 individual preferred securities and baby bonds. Fixed income represents a significant portion of our investment mix, providing much-needed immunity from most market drama. This allows us to focus on a steady income rather than the market drama. Let us now discuss our top investment-grade fixed income picks.

Pick #1: Athene Preferreds and Baby Bond—Up to 7.4% Yields

U.S. annuity sales remain strong, totaling $105.4 billion in the first quarter of 2025. This marks the sixth consecutive quarter with annuity sales topping $100 billion. Athene is a leading retirement services company with over $400 billion of total assets as of June 30, 2025, and operations in the United States, Bermuda, Canada, and Japan. The company is consistently ranked #1 in U.S. annuity sales and is a wholly owned subsidiary of Apollo Global Management (APO). Yet, Athene operates as a separate credit group with its own capital structure and carries A1 / A+ / A+ /A+ ratings by Moody’s, S&P, Fitch, and AM Best, respectively.

Preferred Securities

  • 6.35% Fixed-to-Float, Non-Cumulative, Series A, Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (ATH.PR.A) – Yield 6.3%

  • 5.625% Fixed Rate, Non-Cumulative, Series B, Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (ATH.PR.B) – Yield 6.3%

  • 4.875% Fixed Rate, Non-Cumulative, Series D, Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (ATH.PR.D) – Yield 6.4%

  • 7.750% Rate Reset, Non-Cumulative, Series E, Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (ATH.PR.E) – Yield 7.4%.

Athene ended Q2 with $10.3 billion in cash and cash equivalents and reported $1.4 billion in net income during 1H 2025. This compares to $90 million in preferred dividends paid during the first six months of its fiscal year. In May, Athene redeemed its Series C preferred when it became callable, since this was a rate-reset type that carried a high interest rate of 5Y T-bill + 5.97% per annum, resetting every five years.

At this time, ATH-D presents an excellent fixed-rate bargain at a deep discount, with 6.% QDI and ~32% upside to par. Upcoming rate cuts would serve as a strong tailwind for its market price, making it a good investment now while it is still on sale. However, given its lower coupon, this preferred is unlikely to be redeemed in the near future.

Baby Bond

  • 7.25% Rate-Reset Junior Subordinated Debentures due 3/30/2064 (ATHS) – Yield 7.0%.

Athene also has a baby bond that trades slightly above par, currently offering a 7% yield. This bond is callable in March 2029 (and will likely be redeemed), presenting 3.5 years of steady interest payments.

Pick#2: AFG Baby Bonds—Up to 6.4% Yield

American Financial Group, Inc. (AFG) is a leader in the specialty property and casualty marketplace, with a history tracing back to 1959. The company’s Specialty P&C Group comprises over 35 diversified businesses and operates in three broad segments. Source.

AFG has consistently made it on Ward’s 50 P&C Top Performers and has been rated A (Excellent) or better by AM Best for over 115 years, making it one of only three insurers to carry such stellar industry credentials. For more than 12 consecutive years, AFG has maintained a Combined Ratio below 94%, underscoring the company’s consistent underwriting strength. Combined Ratio is an insurance metric that indicates the profitability of the core business. A high-level calculation would be (Claims Paid + Operating Expenses) / Premiums Collected. If the ratio is below 100%, it means that the company is making an underwriting profit. A ratio above 100% implies the company is paying out more than it collects.

AFG maintains a $16 billion investment portfolio, with 95% of its fixed maturities carrying Investment-Grade ratings.

AFG has delivered substantially better results than the S&P 500 (SP500) over the past 20 years, with dividends being at the forefront of the company’s returns to shareholders.

The company recently delivered a 10% YoY increase to its annual dividend, marking 20 years of consecutive annual dividend growth. The 10-Yr CAGR in AFG’s annual dividend is an impressive 12.3%. AFG’s management has significant skin in the game, with 24% of common shares owned by the founding family, executives, and the company’s retirement plan. This indicates a strong alignment of interests between insiders and shareholders.

During the first six months of FY 2025, AFG generated $3.7 billion in revenues, with $328 million in net earnings. The company spent $38 million on interest expenses during this period and reported $1.2 billion in cash and cash equivalents on its balance sheet.

  • 5.875% Subordinated Debentures due March 30, 2059 (AFGB) – Yield 6.4%

  • 5.125% Subordinated Debentures due December 15, 2059 (AFGC) – Yield 6.2%

  • 5.625% Subordinated Debentures due June 1, 2060 (AFGD) – Yield 6.4%

  • 4.50% Subordinated Debentures due September 15, 2060 (AFGE) – Yield 5.9%.

AFG has four investment-grade BBB+ rated baby bonds, offering up to 6.4% yields, trading at deep discounts to par. These are long-duration bonds, maturing only in 2059-60, implying a higher level of sensitivity to interest rate changes. As of today, all four baby bonds are callable, but it is unlikely that it would happen.

Currently, AFGE in particular offers a 5.9% yield and ~31% upside to par. AFGC yields 6.2% and presents a ~22% upside to par. With relatively lower coupons and higher upside to par, these present attractive investments to collect steady income for several years.

Conclusion

To successfully navigate the financial markets, investors must have a clear understanding of market risks and need to operate with discipline. To be a successful investor, you must operate unemotionally. But humans are emotional beings, making it far easier to preach than practice.

No investment is completely immune to market risks, but high-quality preferreds and baby bonds have the ability to shield the investor from most. Steady income and better immunity to market noise from these securities make them investments that you don’t have to babysit.

At High Dividend Opportunities, our focus on maintaining a diversified fixed-income portfolio is to bring stability and peace of mind through unpredictable markets. This is what you need for a retirement you deserve: a stress-free retirement.

data.title: Roivant: Brepocitinib's DM Data Impress, Pipeline Focus Narrows
data.description:
Roivant Sciences Overview

Roivant Sciences (NASDAQ:ROIV) stock appears to be finally breaking out of a multi-year trading range. The catalyst? Positive Phase 3 data evaluating brepocitinib (“brep”) for the treatment of dermatomyositis (DM).

My last eval of ROIV came in February and previewed its Q4 2024 earnings report, as well as discussed two of their key assets (brep and mosliciguat). Although in previous articles, I had been bullish on ROIV, my patience was admittedly wearing thin.

I did point out brep Phase 2 data in non-infectious uveitis. Roivant’s subsidiary, Priovant, is sponsoring the Phase 3 trial in this indication (CLARITY). So, is it possible that Roivant has another “pipeline in a product” with brep?

The article that follows reviews the latest data and models brep's market potential in DM. Given the stock's recent appreciation, the risk/reward profile in ROIV appears unchanged, meriting an unchanged Hold rating.

Recent Developments

On its website, Priovant describes brep as an “oral once-daily dual inhibitor of TYK2 and JAK1.”

In the Phase 3 (VALOR) study in DM, 30 mg each day achieved a mean Total Improvement Score, or TIS, of 46.5 versus 31.2 for placebo at 52 weeks (p=0.0006). Moreover, all secondary endpoints, such as skin, muscle, and steroid-sparing, also hit. With JAK inhibitors, people are always wary of thromboembolic risk (blood clots). However, for brep, safety was “consistent with prior trials,” with no higher rates of malignancy or MACE (heart attack, stroke) versus placebo. The company intends to submit a U.S. marketing application in 1H26.

The only approved treatment in DM is IVIG/Octagam 10%. However, monthly IVIG is burdensome and expensive. Steroids and immunosuppressants, like methotrexate, are also utilized in DM, but come with many side effects (especially with long-term use) and have mixed efficacy.

VALOR data signals that brep is a differentiated option in DM. An oral, daily option with a safety profile that avoids class issues and allows for the tapering of steroids is a highly attractive proposition.

As for the deal mechanics surrounding Pfizer (PFE) and Roivant in Priovant. Pfizer holds 25% of Priovant, while Roivant controls 74%. Priovant originally licensed brep from Pfizer and controls U.S. and Japan rights. Priovant is pegged for sub-teens royalties to Pfizer on U.S./Japan sales.

It has been estimated that there are around 31,000 U.S. persons with DM. For the following drug DCF calculator, I assumed that half of them will be eligible for systemic treatment. I used a peak share of 33% if efficacy/label is strong and payers prefer oral over IVIG burden or off-label treatment. Small-molecule immunology tends to be ~$80k WAC (e.g., RINVOQ, SOTYKTU), but I went with $70k to account for rebates.

So, I have peak annual U.S. sales of $543M. A rule of thumb is that ROW sales are 1x U.S. sales. While there are more people ROW, net prices are typically lower and access lags. In any event, we are looking at a potential blockbuster in DM. In fact, I think this puppy is worth at least $3 billion at this moment, with Roivant’s share being $2.2 billion. Given Roivant’s history (it recently exited two commercial-stage assets at strong terms), it may be interested in selling its share. In any event, it gives it a notable commercial asset, which is nice to have if you’re running a pharma company, right?

Outside of Priovant, Immunovant (IMVT) continues to advance batoclimab in Graves’ disease, as well as 1402 in a variety of autoimmune conditions. I have written on Immunovant in the past (see article). 1402 is Immunovant’s “economic engine” so long as safety/LDL profile stays clean. It will also have to deal with some competition in that space. Meanwhile, Pulmovant’s mosliciguat remains in a Phase 2 study for pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease. I briefly touched on mosliciguat's prospects in PH-ILD in my last article on ROIV (click here).

The Bottom Line

To wrap up, brep’s win provides Roivant with another savvy commercial-stage (NDA preparations) drug. Moving forward, Roivant is concentrated on brep (near-term) and 1402 (medium-term) with $4.5 billion in cash and ST investments to bridge both. So, execution in the next 18-24 months is mainly 1) brep filing/label/access, 2) 1402 enrollment pace + first late-stage readouts, (3) capital deployment (BD vs more buybacks).

If one thing is for certain, it is that execution is paramount for Roivant. I mentioned the enormous cash pile, but the company is “burning” (cash from operations) ~$200 million every quarter. Moreover, the company is graciously buying back its own shares – having just completed a $1.5 billion program.

From my porch, I’m still at odds with Roivant’s “vant” model (which is somewhat dependent on replicating “Telavant-like success”), and the market’s ~7% price appreciation of ROIV post-VALOR appears to account for brep's increased probability of success (55% → 90%). So, I’d like to hold steady on ROIV (maintain Hold)

data.title: Coherent: Building Momentum Across AI And Industrial Demand
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My investment idea is that Coherent's (NYSE:COHR) unique position at the intersection of several fast-growing markets (optical components for AI data centers and industrial lasers), while optimizing the business, creates the basis for faster financial growth in 2026. Due to the boom in demand for high-speed optical transceivers for AI-centric data centers (revenue in this segment increased by 61% over the year) and a focus on more profitable areas, Coherent demonstrates accelerated revenue growth and a strong operational breakthrough. The market does not sufficiently reflect this dynamic yet: the shares are trading at about 22x the projected profit, which is even at a slight discount to the sector, despite the projected revenue growth of 10% per year. In my opinion, upcoming drivers (new products for data centers, expansion in the industrial laser industry), combined with improved margins, can lead to a revaluation of stocks. I am assigning a "Buy" recommendation with a target price of $116 (12.8% upside), as I believe that the current price underestimates the long-term potential of Coherent and the effect of the ongoing strategic focus on key growth markets.

Shaping the Next Phase of Photonics Leadership

Coherent Corp. today represents a true photonic giant with revenue of $5.8 billion. The business consists of three segments. Coherent is one of the leaders in the production of optical components and modules for telecommunications and data centers (the legacy of the absorbed Finisar). The company also develops and supplies a wide range of lasers for industry (fiber, solid-state, ultraviolet, etc.), applicable in the automotive industry, microelectronics, and medical equipment. The segment historically inherited from II-VI includes compound semiconductors, as well as infrared optical components. Revenue is generated in all segments by selling equipment, materials, and licenses to large B2B clients: from technology giants (Hyperscalers, telecom equipment manufacturers) to the industrial sector and defense contractors.

In 2025, an agreement was signed to sell the Aerospace & Defense division for $400 million to free up resources and reduce debt. The share of this business in revenue is small ($20 million annually), but its sale highlighted Coherent's strategy to focus on the most promising areas of photonics (such as data centers and industrial automation) and improve the balance sheet. In addition, the company accelerated the launch of new products: OCS, a revolutionary liquid crystal optical switch for data centers, has begun sales, which can significantly increase the addressable market (according to Coherent, OCS will add more than $2 billion to possible revenue in data centers by 2030).

The company's key competitors include Lumentum Holdings (optical transceivers for networks and lasers for 3D scanners, with $1.7 billion in revenue), IPG Photonics (which dominates fiber lasers for industry), nLight (a specialist in defense laser diodes) and a number of Asian optics manufacturers. Compared to its competitors, Coherent stands out for its size and breadth of product range: in fact, it is a one-stop photonics store with a global scale, but focused specifically on optical technologies. This combination allows the company to benefit from several market trends at once - from the growth of traffic in AI networks to production automation - and flexibly redistribute resources between segments.

FY2025: Scaling Revenues, Stabilizing Earnings

Coherent ended fiscal 2025 on a positive note: Q4 FY2025 revenue reached a record $1.53 billion (+16.4% YoY), slightly exceeding market expectations. This growth was primarily driven by a surge in the network and communications solutions segment: sales in the Networking area increased by +39% YoY in Q4, due to strong demand from cloud data centers and telecom companies. In FY2025, Coherent achieved record revenue of $5.81 billion, which is 23% higher than the previous year.

Thanks to revenue growth and optimization programs, the profitability of the business has improved. Non-GAAP operating profit for Q4 was $275 million, an increase of +35.8% YoY with an operating margin of 18%. For the full year, non-GAAP EBIT reached $1.04 billion (margin of 17.8% versus 13.1% a year earlier).

In FY2025, the company recorded a small GAAP loss of $0.52 per share, while adjusted non-GAAP EPS was $3.53 (an increase of $2.32 YoY). The key factor in this gap is large depreciation charges (from acquisitions II-VI/Finisar/Coherent) and payments on preferred shares, which reduce profits according to GAAP. It is important to note that Coherent has already achieved steady non-GAAP earnings, and GAAP losses in reporting are gradually decreasing as the business is integrated (for comparison, a year earlier the GAAP loss per share was $1.84). I expect the gap between GAAP and adjusted figures to narrow in fiscal year 2026 as the lion's share of merger synergies are realized and acquisition depreciation rates begin to decrease.

Positioning for Acceleration in 2H FY2026

Coherent's management is quite confident about the new financial year. For Q1 FY2026, the revenue forecast is in the range of $1.46-1.60 billion and non-GAAP EPS of $0.93-1.13. At first glance, the revenue range suggests -4% - +5% to the Q4 level (the average point of $1.53 billion is approximately equal to the last quarter), which looks conservative. In fact, management's forecast implies continued double-digit growth in comparable assets. There is no official figure for the entire FY2026, but on Investor Day 2025, the company announced a target for average annual growth of 10-15% until 2030, and I estimate that in the coming year revenue may exceed $6.2 billion (+8-9% yoy) - slightly lower than the record +23% in 2025, which is explained by more a high base and the effect of selling non-core units.

The growth drivers have not gone away: the development of AI infrastructure is only gaining momentum, and Coherent, in fact, is selling "shovels for the AI gold rush." In the second half of FY2026, I expect results to accelerate, by which time new products (such as OCS switches and 1.6T/3.2T transceivers) will begin to make a more tangible contribution to revenue. My forecast for Q3 FY2026 (the quarter that will reflect the already mature demand of 2026) is revenue in the region of $1.6-1.65 billion and non-GAAP EPS in the order of $1.10-1.20. In other words, I expect 15-20% annual growth in the network solutions segment to continue as new data centers are deployed and the overall gross margin will increase closer to 38-39% due to a better product range. Investors should be guided by the fact that Coherent may exceed consensus expectations in the second half of FY2026 if trends (AI demand, telecom growth, industrial order recovery in China) persist.

Earnings Growth Not Fully Priced In

My estimate is based on a DCF model with an unlevered FCF for the period 2025-2030. I expect Coherent's revenue to grow by an average of 8-9% per year, reaching about $9.2 billion by 2030, with optical solutions for data centers being the main driver of growth, while mature segments will grow moderately. I have included an EBIT margin of 10-11% in the model, which reflects the effect of scaling and optimizing costs, while maintaining the EBITDA margin at 18-19%. Capital expenditures, according to my estimates, will remain elevated in the coming years (8% of revenue) and then decrease to 7.5% when key capacities are put into operation, and the need for net working capital will be about 2-3% of sales growth. As a result, Coherent will generate free cash flow with an FCF Yield of 8-9% on average.

I discounted the forecast FCF for WACC at 10.64% due to the high share capital and a very high five-year beta at 2.01 (all other prerequisites are shown in the table above).

Coherent shares are currently priced quite attractively relative to the sector. The company's forward P/E for the next 12 months is 22.5x, which is slightly lower than the median value for the Hardware/Photonics sector (24x) and almost at the level of its own 5-year average. Given the expected profit growth (PEG ratio 0.8 versus 1.8 for the sector), the market clearly does not set a premium multiplier for Coherent's AI exposure - on the contrary, PEG indicates an underestimation of growth by almost 50% relative to analogues. The situation is similar in terms of revenue and EBITDA: EV/Sales FWD 3.5x and EV/EBITDA FWD 14.8x are almost at parity with typical companies in the industry. This means that investors value Coherent close to the market average, despite its leadership in a number of niches and higher expected growth. I would like to note that historically, shares of II-VI/Coherent have traded cheaper (5-year median P/E 22 and EV/EBITDA 12), but then the business had a smaller scale and lower profitability. Finally, it is worth considering that Coherent's reporting (TTM) indicators are distorted by integration costs, for example. GAAP P/E LTM is untenable due to a loss, and P/CF is 25x higher than normal due to temporarily low operating cash flow. Investors are probably focusing on future earnings, where Coherent has significant improvement.

Stock valuation based on terminal value calculation multipliers through P/E and EV/EBITDA. According to the P/E method, even a conservative scenario with a multiplier of 22.0x gives a fair price of about $116 per share, which implies an upside of about 12.9% from current levels. The EV/EBITDA method demonstrates even more convincing dynamics. Both approaches indicate that the market has overly conservative expectations about growth rates and Coherent's ability to generate profits, while the fundamental drivers (growth of data centers, new products in the field of optics and reduction of debt burden) allow us to expect a revaluation. Based on the results, I assign a Buy rating to Coherent shares with a base target price of $116 per share, which implies a comfortable upside of 12.8%.

Capital Returns Deferred Pending Deleveraging

Coherent does not yet have a direct return of capital to shareholders. The company does not pay dividends on ordinary shares and, according to management, does not plan to start until the leverage is significantly reduced. This is due to both the strategy of reinvesting profits in growth (R&D 10% of revenue, promising M&A) and the limitations of debt covenants - loan agreements usually prohibit the payment of dividends or share buybacks with high debt.

There is no buyback program for ordinary shares now (and there was no buyback program in FY2025). In my opinion, this approach is justified: until the Net Debt/EBITDA metric falls below 2x, it is better to spend every extra dollar on reducing interest on debts than on symbolic dividends. In the long term, as debt decreases and profits grow, Coherent will be able to consider returning to payments to shareholders - for example, by 2027-2028, it is possible to launch a modest buyback from excess cash flow.

Risks

Coherent has a number of significant risks specific to high-tech companies. Despite the debt reduction, the debt/EBITDA metric remains at 2.5x, which makes the business sensitive to rates and profit fluctuations. Integration of II-VI and Coherent takes time, and disruptions in synergies or new product launches (such as 1.6T transceivers and OCS) can affect margins and revenue. Additional risks are associated with cyclical demand and competition in the telecom and laser segments, possible technology obsolescence, as well as geopolitics and export restrictions, especially in China. Nevertheless, a diversified portfolio of clients and industries reduces the company's vulnerability to problems in certain areas.

Final Thoughts

Coherent Corp. offers a rare combination: It is a large and well-established technology company that is simultaneously demonstrating good growth rates due to new AI and optics markets. My analysis shows that the business is successfully transforming - financial metrics are improving, and long-term revenue growth of 8-9%+ per year with profit expansion is ahead. I believe that the positive drivers (the boom in AI data centers, new products, cost optimization) outweigh the identified risks. Collectively, Coherent is worthy of a "Buy" rating with the potential to increase the share price to $116.

data.title: AI Meets Border Control: BigBear.ai Is On Time To The Party
data.description:
Investment thesis

BigBear.ai (NYSE:BBAI) stock is once again up, rising just over 4% so far in pre-market today. Last week, we saw their veriScan biometric technology get implemented at Nashville International Airport, and a number of tailwinds point to a larger adoption of this tech across other airports. In my previous coverage, I tipped BigBear to win a significant US border biometric contract under the new budget from the Big Beautiful Bill that set aside $673 million for biometrics at border protection. And while it’s still very early to see that money get spent, there are other positive signs I’m looking at for this stock. We’re seeing an increasing number of airports undergo modernization upgrades with improved Customs and Border Protection [CBP] processes. Nashville, before the upgrade, was actually rated as one of the worst airports in the US in terms of wait time, as I'll explain. So it was no surprise that they got automated biometric processes with veriScan. The good news for investors is that there is no shortage of airports across the US and Canada that are in desperate need of upgrades to reduce congestion and wait times. I’ve tipped a few of these airports to be next in line to receive BigBear's tech. What makes me extra bullish on these potential upcoming contacts is that there are several airports that have already filed for grants for the modernization of CBP FIS (Federal Inspection Station) points. In my experience, we often see a revamp of points of entry systems and switches to biometrics alongside these upgrades. They present an optimal time for airports to switch out their tech. As I dig into the number of airports that require this digital transformation, I see new tailwinds for BigBear in this direction. The stock has recently passed over the $5 mark and is trading in the mid $5 range pre-market today. After the Nashville news, the stock jumped roughly 5%, so I think another airport adoption in the near term could significantly add momentum to the stock in this sense, as we see more and more airport/point of entry infrastructure adopt veriScan.

Biometric tailwinds

Last week, we saw BigBear roll out their Enhanced Passenger Processing (EPP) system at Nashville International Airport. The tech is again powered by their veriScan biometrics system, which is tipped to speed up the processing time for passengers since physical passport presentation is skipped in the majority of passenger cases. As a quick recap, the veriScan system works by capturing a live facial image of the traveler at the point of entry. The photo is then matched against existing government records in real time. If the match is successful, eligible US citizens can bypass presenting their physical passport and proceed through. But anyone flagged for discrepancies is referred to an officer for additional screening. The news was good for investors, and the stock initially jumped by more than 5% that day. However, the best part of seeing this for me was that BigBear is rolling out its tech for civilian infrastructure rather than for government defense. If BigBear wants to grow, it needs better diversification of its tech, and the best way to do that is to push it into everyday living. Historically, they’ve leaned on defense and intelligence contracts, but the rollout into more airports is getting the stock a lot of new traction recently. It's also a recurring market that won't go away, unlike what we’re seeing with defense, in which stocks rise and fall depending on global conflicts.

With every airport or point of entry deployment, like at Nashville, the relationship with US Customs and Border Protection gets ever so slightly stronger. What we want is more federal contracts across US airports. Now, post-pandemic, with geopolitical tensions rising, we’re seeing a big political divide across the US. And no matter what side you’re with, the tailwinds are pushing the country towards tighter security. It's becoming more and more of a top US federal agenda item. We’re seeing Washington pour billions into modernizing border protection and TSA at airports. I put together a quick table displaying some of the spending tailwinds for national security. At the same time, we’re seeing a big shift from manual to automated systems. Populations are growing, and one way to manage the masses is to automate as many processes as possible. Now, AI-powered biometric identity systems address both the efficiency and the security aspects at travel points.

So here is where BigBear fits in. So far, veriScan has been deployed in at least 12 US and Canadian airports. Examples include Charlotte, Chicago O’Hare, Cross Border Xpress, Dallas Fort Worth, Denver, JFK (Terminals 4 & 8), LAX (Terminal 7, Tom Bradley), Montreal-Trudeau, Port of Seattle, and Vancouver Fraser Port Authority. Now, we don’t know exactly how much revenue translates from each airport deployment; however, there are a few clues that I like to look at. The company operated through two major segments: 1. cyber/engineering, which includes offerings like systems engineering, cybersecurity, cloud engineering, and network operations, and 2. analytics, which covers predictive analytics, prescriptive analytics, data aggregation & interpretation, and decision-support tools. Now, the majority of their revenue comes from a couple of big clients, the US Department of Defense and Homeland and Border Security. So far, the backlog is up to $380 million. The way I see their revenue breakdown is that government and defense contracts make up the majority of that revenue, though their cyber and engineering arc. Given the efficiency disruptions from Army programs that are already impacting 2Q25 revenue, the segment carries significant weight on earnings. Secondly, I think their analytics program is a core offering, and makes up the second largest share. That then brings me to airport rollouts and their biometric veriScan system. I put this as their fastest-growing revenue driver, but not yet their largest. However, airport contracts make up a significant share of the backlog I mentioned.

Who is next after Nashville?

In my previous coverage, I tipped the company to be awarded significant contracts at the Mexico border, with Trump ramping up spending for biometrics at the border. $673 million is specifically set aside for biometric systems, but it's still too early to see that play out. So for now, investors should look to a continued rollout of airport biometric systems and continued adoption on this front. So Nashville was the latest to get the upgrade, but did anything point to this beforehand? Well, a recent report back in June tipped Nashville as one of the top 3 worst US airports for international travel. They blamed it on the long wait at border protection lines, with an average wait time of 31.1 minutes. On top of this, the airport has been expanding its international flights with new routes to Europe. So, along with this, the airport has already taken steps toward modernization. They had already undertaken an expansion of their main lobby, indicating a willingness to grow their infrastructure. In my opinion, airports that get an automated upgrade will most likely share several of these traits, and there's no shortage of them across the US and Canada. For example, Miami International Airport frequently shows up as having the worst wait times and is seeing increased international traffic. LAX is under constant pressure to improve arrival processing times and is already showing good signs in that it's using CBP time-saving tools.

However, there's something else that points towards more airports getting an upgrade. What I’ve been keeping a close eye on is airports that have published modernization or CBP FIS (Federal Inspection Station) upgrade grants this year. If we take everything I mentioned, here are the best near-term candidates to adopt BigBear’s VeriScan next. It's the kind of modernization that usually comes with a biometric tech push. Firstly, I see that Tallahassee airport has just been awarded roughly $2 million to build a CBP-approved port of entry and FIS in their new 40,000 square foot terminal expansion. In my experience, new FIS builds are key fits for spec in biometric facial processing. Secondly, Syracuse Hancock Airport was awarded about $20 million for two projects, which include upgrades to the CBP FIS. The New York Governor’s office also highlighted getting FIS modernization up to the latest CBP standards. That’s a clear trigger for biometric deployments. Even if we don't see these exact airports I mentioned adopt BigBear's tech, the good news is that there is no shortage of airports in need of a biometrics upgrade. Multiple airports have fresh or expanded FIS projects, which, for me, is the natural window to specify biometric entry/exit lanes. We also see high international expected volumes as key drivers for airports to file for upgrade grants. I made a table below for my next picks to get a BigBear veriScan upgrade.

Risks

Now, there has obviously been a lot of strong momentum around biometrics at the airport, with the stock shifting 5% after the Nashville news. However, the company remains a high-risk investment. It's no secret that BigBear is still heavily reliant on large government and defense contracts, which can be very unpredictable and subject to political shifts. Geopolitical tensions in Europe are a big driver of defense stocks, and when news comes of a potential peace deal, it can act as a strong headwind for the stock. While the veriScan rollout at airports is promising and shifts their revenue narrative, away from government contracts, adoption timelines can be slow, as airports must secure funding in what is still a shaky macro environment. It can be several quarters between an airport securing funding grants and veriScan being deployed. Execution risk is also material. BigBear needs to prove it can scale biometric deployments profitably. We are still in the early days in terms of the airport rollout we are seeing.

Valuation

BigBear right now is being valued almost entirely on forward revenue momentum. That's why we're seeing the stock trade at quite a premium. As per Seeking Alpha, the stock is trading on a forward price-to-sales ratio of 14.13 vs. a sector median of just 3.42, a 313% premium. Similarly, the forward EV to sales ratio sits at 12.05 against a sector of 3.41, a 253% premium. Now, let's layer in a net cash cushion of roughly $278 million, since cash for BigBear is at roughly $391 million with about $113 million in debt. Take this figure along with an EV of about $1.61 billion, and the message is clear for me: the market is expecting visible growth and a mix shift toward higher-margin revenue. Investors are expecting BigBear’s revenue portfolio to consist of more software tied to federal and critical-infrastructure demand, such as that of biometrics at points of entry. Investors are also likely pricing in a credible path to EBITDA breakeven in the near term. Scale is clearly improving, and we’re seeing policy tailwinds such as CBP/TSA/airport modernization that is expanding their TAM. So in short, these valuation multiples we’re seeing are begging for recurring contracts and more operating leverage to be justified. I’m expecting a re-rate of the stock soon, and I put it down to airport adoption of veriScan. This is the cleanest near-term catalyst.

What's Next?

Going forward, I expect the stock momentum to continue. We’re likely to see 3Q25 results sometime in the first week of November, and while their veriScan airport rollout may be their biggest revenue grower, we need to see increased airport adoption show up on earnings.

data.title: ISCF, The Multifactor Tilt That Overturns The Ex-US Large Caps
data.description:
And if I told you that outside the US I prefer to focus on small cap segment rather than large cap?

Well, it must be said that overseas, small caps play perhaps a more decisive role for the international economic fabric, especially in Europe. And mind you, I say this as a European analyst.

However, it is not a segment that is so easy to ride: it is full of inefficiencies, and not by chance active managers continue to compete well with ETFs (despite the prohibitive costs).

Here, I must admit though that I found an excellent meeting point, between accessible costs and quant-based selection of holdings, in iShares MSCI Intl Small-Cap Multifactor ETF (NYSEARCA:ISCF). Because it introduces a multifactor approach, very functional in my opinion for this market, especially after the February 2023 update.

ISCF

The fund has a TER of 0.23% and a contained bid-ask spread of 0.07%.

Well, I start right away with costs, because a first objective of my research is to find a fund that does screening, but at an accessible TER (distinctive compared to the logic of hedge or mutual funds).

And ISCF offers a competitive TER, and honestly I do not quite understand how the AUM can still remain today in the low range of $360M. It must be said that the fund, although it has an established track record, changed its reference benchmark only on February 28, 2023, moving from MSCI world ex USA Small Cap Diversified Multiple-Factor to the STOXX International Small-Cap Equity Factor Index.

What does the fund do?

Once past the hurdle of costs, a screening that is at least filtered is needed, if not active, then at least quant based. And on ISCF the logic is that of a multifactor portfolio. And I will be honest, even before scrutinizing ISCF’s multifactor structure, the idea that behind the fund there is such an approach had appealed to me. As a scholar and enthusiast of Fama & French’s factor models, I think that factors, if well integrated into the portfolio, can replace, even where the market is more dispersive, active managers in an efficient way.

The multi-factor logic of ISCF

In the meantime, we are in the field of international small cap selection ex US, and let me say, also ex emerging markets.

Then, the factors used in the multi-factor logic are value, quality, momentum, low size, low volatility while maintaining a “blended” approach.

value
momentum
Size
low volatility
quality

It then introduces constraints on sectors/countries compared to the Parent Index, a 10% limit per single security and a cumulative cap of 22.5% for weights >4.5%.

The result

The dominant weight is Europe, because if we add up the countries within the geographical distribution we get an overall exposure of more than 30%. Then follows Japan with 26%. Then, an intriguing element, Canada comes next (8.7%) as an “outlier,” an element I appreciate: many small cap ex-US ETFs do not include Canada, ISCF does. This adds an energy/materials tilt (given the sector weight in Canada).

The multifactor selection results in 1,048 securities, where the Top holdings all have weights below 1% and, surprisingly, an interesting sector distribution: the top two sectors are industrials and financials, an element that reflects the typical composition of developed ex-US small caps, with less exposure to tech compared to the US. Interesting as a geographical counterbalance.

In technical terms

the factor optimization is strongly “de-risked”: the direct effect is first of all a more stable tracking, but diluted single-factor alpha, an element that is not necessarily a negative aspect. In fact, volatility is well under control. The factor combination (low vol, quality) reduces risk compared to the typical small cap profile: std dev 16.7% and beta 0.84, which is a “paradox.” An element that I appreciate very much and that is distinctive compared, first of all, to the global small cap segment, but also compared to US value.

An unusual comparison

Let’s quickly make a comparison with VGK (European large caps).

  • First of all, we notice how ISCF maintains a competitive tendency compared to the large cap segment (VGK), confirming the idea that the ex-US economic fabric is much more anchored to small caps.

  • But ISCF, simply, also has A grades in the Risk section, a distinctive element even compared to European large caps (known for being prevalently value).

  • If we look at the total return, we finally realize that a strong stabilizing element in this sense is also having a competitive dividend, today at 3.86%, close to 4%, therefore double that of US small caps, and also higher than European large caps.

Risk

Surely, I would put Japan risk in first place: an element that is also isolated by BlackRock’s own prospectus, highlighting issues of public debt, demographics, energy imports, geopolitical relations, natural disasters. Given the typical overweighting of Japan in ex-US small caps, here it is a concrete risk driver. However, I also want to emphasize that from a portfolio perspective, rather than short-term risk, it is in my opinion more rewarding to participate in the Japanese market through the small cap segment, and perhaps through an ETF that introduces a multifactor approach.

Comparing it with EWJ, both in terms of volatility and total return, we have confirmation in this sense.

Conclusion

Maybe it is an extremist view, but I think that when we look outside the US, the focus should increasingly shift towards the small cap segment, because it seems to be very representative of the economic fabric of countries. But working on this sector is not easy. The introduction of a multifactor approach like that of ISCF helps to isolate within the vastness the most deserving companies, dampening volatility, while keeping the return competitive compared to the large cap segment.

data.title: Roblox's Platform Is Maturing Faster Than Wall Street Thinks
data.description:
I have been keeping an eye on Roblox (NYSE:RBLX) for a while now. The company is still perceived as a kid’s game, which I believe is too reductive. The reality seems to be much different, with the platform becoming more like an infrastructure for social gaming and virtual economies. The most recent financial results seem to prove that a shift is underway. Let’s dissect this.

Booking growth and monetization optionality

In 2Q25, the bookings were $1.44B, something not seen many times in companies this size, representing a massive year-over-year growth of 51%, while the DAU growth was 41%, this being the main indicator that the monetization is expanding. Bear in mind that the bookings are the true demand signal, representing the cash received for Robux and other subscriptions. The fact that bookings are growing faster than DAU means that this is a monetization unlock story.

The FCF metrics support our assertion that the monetization is being unlocked. We can dig deeper into a more granular level to unpack a couple of dynamics that are tailwinds to monetization. For starters, the virtual economy with in-app Robux purchases is increasing ARPPU. The ad-based monetization in rewarded video is integrated with Google’s ads and is scaling fast. On another note, creators can now license their IP, a bit like other creator-brand collaborations on YouTube. Finally, AI tools are now available, allowing for rapid goods creation, thus reducing time to market.

Global expansion as a growth engine

What most people aren’t paying attention to is how fast they are scaling on the international front, and the upside this can unleash in the next 2 to 3 years. The company’s business model is heavily dependent on getting people on the platform and keeping them engaged to eventually drive some form of monetization. Basically, international users are going through the same funnel as the US, just maybe 2 or 3 years behind.

A couple of recent improvements are having a huge impact on driving international growth. The burst of data centers capacity in Asia is helping to get lower latency, and auto translation is unlocking the potential in countries like Japan.

Monetization efficiency and creator flywheel

One of the most overlooked metrics is average bookings per daily user growth. This metric is growing at 20% year over year. Basically, this tells us that users are spending more. However, developers are also gaining more, with developers’ payouts now growing at 52%. That means that developers can make real money in the platform, which creates a virtuous cycle where better monetization attracts more creators, making better games, which should improve monetization.

Valuation

To give a proper look at valuation, we need to select a peer set that captures the many dimensions of the platform, and to be honest, there isn’t a completely direct comparable. In my opinion, the closest comparables are Unity (U), Duolingo (DUOL), Snap (SNAP), Meta (META), and YouTube (Google) (GOOG)(GOOGL). Now, the problems begin because when you look at the valuation for Roblox and peers, we get the sense that it is insane.

If you look at the table, the company is trading at 97x price to FCF and 59x EV to FCF. I agree, this looks crazy. However, there are a few key reasons. First, FCF just inflected. The company went from burning cash to generating cash, and its FCF margins are still expanding. Therefore, this is a work in progress. On the other hand, bookings growth is at 51% YoY, while FCF is lagging this metric because of deferred revenue accounting. Therefore, we can expect the valuation to re-anchor if the company keeps hitting the high end of the guidance. And this is not far-fetched, since the company is now monetizing a very sticky user base.

Risks

One of the major risks the platform has been exposed to is predator concerns. As I keep following the company, these issues keep popping up periodically, making it almost feel like a time bomb. Even with a big effort in deploying trust and safety mechanisms, we are still hit with periodic media firestorms. And this is also likely to attract regulators’ attention. And make no mistake, more regulations will mean slower growth.

Another issue that I keep seeing mentioned by analysts is the over-reliance on a few big hits. To thrive, the whole ecosystem depends on having a mid-tier segment of games that is visible enough to keep the developers engaged in creating games. If only a couple of top hits end up getting most of the rewards, the ecosystem might dry up fast. On the other hand, one of the most interesting features that creates ecosystem momentum is user-generated content. The problem is that with UGC growth also comes the possibility of IP infringements. In the end, it is unlikely that lawsuits on this might destroy the company, but it is likely that it will divert resources, and it might cool down the ecosystem momentum.

The ad monetization is still a big uncertainty. Yes, they already have ad initiatives like Rewarded Video, but these initiatives are still in beta. This is not yet a meaningful revenue stream. The market seems to be already baking in some of that optionality, and if the results disappoint, the stock price will likely take a hit.

Finally, the high valuation leaves no room for error, meaning that any miss on the metrics we have covered (DAU, ABPDAU, FCF) might lead to a painful re-rating.

Investment Action

The company is trading at very rich multiples, and that makes it not for the faint of heart. However, if you zoom out, you can see that this is not a kids’ game anymore. We can distill the company’s ethos as a global platform for interactive entertainment. A kind of YouTube meets Fortnite.

The company is now reaping the benefits from developing a great discovery engine and creator tools that have unleashed a thriving economy and are now allowing the platform to break out organically. Yes, there are legitimate risks in terms of content moderation and high valuation, but at this pace, it is likely that the market will reward the company with an even higher market cap.

The revenue estimates show exactly that. This is a company with a lot baked in, but if the growth materializes, and the current platform performance seems to support this, then in a few years, the current valuation won’t look that expensive.

data.title: FSK KKR Capital: A Dividend Cut May Be Closer Than I Anticipated
data.description:
Introduction

With a weaker jobs report, it's apparent that we are likely to see multiple rate cuts by the end of the year.

As a result, the BDC (BIZD) sector is likely to face further selling pressure as dividend coverage becomes a focal point for many.

One BDC in particular, FSK KKR Capital Corp. (NYSE:FSK) has faced their fair share of headwinds since the rate hike cycle began.

However, the BDC had seemingly gotten these under control with improvements until their most recent quarter, where non-performing loans rose due to distressed borrowers.

With us expected to see multiple rate cuts by the end of 2025, investors should remain cautious about FSK's dividend sustainability, as I could see a cut before the year is up, or by the first quarter of 2026.

In this article, I discuss FSK's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why a dividend cut may be closer than I previously anticipated.

Previous Thesis

I last covered FSK KKR Capital back in July in an article titled: A Risky Yield, But I Think The Dividend Remains Safe for 2025.

Although I cautioned investors about FSK's yield and headwinds from lower rates, their improved credit quality, robust liquidity, and higher dividend income gave me confidence in their ability to sustain their dividend, at least for the near-term. Since, the stock is down double-digits while the S&P (SP500) is up over 6%.

Deteriorating Financials

FSK KKR Capital reported their second quarter earnings and experienced a decline in their financials. Net investment income fell $0.05 from the prior quarter to $0.62. On an adjusted basis, this also fell $0.05, from $0.65 to $0.60.

Their top line was down $2 million over the same period to $298 million. This was a result of lower interest income from a rise in non-accruals and lower fee income.

During the quarter, FSK successfully removed two companies from the list, but this was offset by the addition of 4 borrowers to non-accrual status. This is a result of tariff impacts & softer consumer demand. Unfortunately, 3 of these are FSK's larger borrowers.

But on a positive note, management stated that their estimated tariff impact has fallen modestly from 8% to the low to mid-single digit range. As expected, these realized losses resulted in a sizable decline in FSK's NAV.

This fell $1.44 quarter-over-quarter to $21.93. Year-over-year, NAV fell 8.3%. For comparison, the chart below shows FSK's NAV performance vs some of their larger peers in the sector.
